narration.default: Lists hold many values in order. Today you'll learn the exact ways to reach in and grab one item, the last item, or a whole chunk in the middle. The same tricks work on strings — every character is at a position, just like every item in a list. Two new ideas: indexing (one position at a time) and slicing (a range of positions).

Type:

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango", "kiwi", "grape"]

Five items, each at a position. Python counts from ZERO — apple is at 0, banana at 1, mango at 2, kiwi at 3, grape at 4. The first item is always [0], not [1].

Type:

print(fruits[0])
print(fruits[2])

The square brackets after the list reach IN to one position. fruits[0] is apple, fruits[2] is mango. Negative indices count from the END — fruits[-1] is grape (the last), fruits[-2] is kiwi (second-last). Try those too if you like.

Type:

print(fruits[1:4])

The colon between two numbers makes a slice — give back a NEW list from the first index UP TO BUT NOT INCLUDING the second. So fruits[1:4] gives banana, mango, kiwi — positions 1, 2, 3. Position 4 is left out. The "up to but not including" rule is consistent everywhere in Python — once you accept it, slicing becomes second nature.

Type:

print(fruits[:3])
print(fruits[2:])

Leave a side blank to mean "from the start" or "to the end". fruits[:3] is the first three (apple, banana, mango). fruits[2:] is mango, kiwi, grape — from position 2 to the end. fruits[:] is the whole list copied — the most common Python idiom for "give me a fresh copy".

Type:

word = "Python"
print(word[0])
print(word[1:4])

Indexing and slicing work on strings too — every character is at a position, just like a one-letter list. word[0] is P. word[1:4] is yth — three characters starting at position 1, up to but not including 4. Same rule, same syntax.
Click Run. The terminal shows apple, mango, ['banana', 'mango', 'kiwi'], ['apple', 'banana', 'mango'], ['mango', 'kiwi', 'grape'], P, and yth. Seven different ways to look INTO the same data.

Use a single index when you want ONE item — the third question in a quiz, the last enemy in a level. Use a slice when you want a CHUNK — the top three scores, the first half of a story, the suffix of a filename. Negative indices are perfect for "last" and "second to last". The slice rule is always start ≤ index < stop — never include the stop.
